uses: Leaves and bark used in traditional remedies for malaria, skin conditions, and as a natural insect repellent. Called 'mwarobaini' (the forty) for its reputed ability to treat forty different ailments.
prep: Leaves boiled as a tea, or bark ground into a paste for topical use.
notes: One of the most common medicinal trees in East African homesteads; also planted for shade.

uses: Leaves eaten as a vegetable and used as a nutritional supplement, rich in vitamins. Traditionally used to support lactation and manage blood pressure.
prep: Leaves cooked like spinach, or dried and ground into powder.
notes: Nicknamed the 'miracle tree' for its dense nutrient profile.

uses: Gel from the leaves applied to burns, cuts, and skin irritation. Also used in some households for hair care.
prep: Leaf cut open and gel applied directly to skin.
notes: Easy to grow in pots; needs well-drained soil.

uses: Leafy vegetable cooked as a side dish, a staple green in many Tanzanian meals. High in iron and vitamins.
prep: Leaves washed, chopped, and cooked with onion, tomato, and a little oil.
notes: Several wild varieties are foraged rather than planted.

uses: Fruit pulp eaten fresh or made into juice, high in vitamin C. Bark used traditionally for fever.
prep: Fruit pulp soaked in water and strained to make a tangy drink.
notes: Iconic tree of the savanna; can live for over a thousand years.

uses: Fruit and sap used for food and traditional palm wine; leaves woven into mats and baskets.
prep: Sap tapped from the flower stalk; fruit eaten raw when young.
notes: An important source of building material as well as food.

uses: The dried flower bud used as a spice, and traditionally chewed or applied for toothache relief.
prep: Dried whole or ground into powder for cooking and remedies.
notes: Zanzibar is historically one of the world's major clove producers.

uses: Fruit eaten fresh or juiced; leaves sometimes used in traditional teas for colds.
prep: Fruit peeled and eaten, or juiced.
notes: Common in home gardens for shade and fruit.

uses: Fruit used traditionally in skin preparations; tree valued ornamentally for its distinctive hanging fruit.
prep: Fruit sliced and fermented before topical use; not eaten raw as it is toxic unprocessed.
notes: Fruit can weigh several kilograms and hangs on long stalks.

uses: Grown purely for its vivid coloured bracts as a hedge or fence plant; no significant medicinal or food use.
prep: Propagated from cuttings; needs pruning to control spread.
notes: Thorny — often planted as a living security fence.

uses: Fruit a staple food eaten ripe or cooked green; leaves used to wrap food for steaming.
prep: Green bananas boiled or roasted; ripe fruit eaten raw.
notes: A dietary staple in much of northern Tanzania, including Kilimanjaro region.

uses: Leaves processed and brewed as the everyday hot beverage, often with milk and spices.
prep: Leaves dried, then boiled or steeped in hot water.
notes: Grown commercially in cooler highland areas.
